title: Daily Briefing — Thursday, June 25
tldr: Web-design play of the day: animate a design on the Figma canvas — then copy the real CSS/React easing straight out of Dev Mode, no After Effects round-trip. At Config 2026 (Jun 24) Figma shipped Figma Motion: a real animation timeline as a fourth canvas mode next to Design / Draw / Dev. You keyframe position, scale, rotation, opacity, drop fade/move/scale presets, define custom easing as reusable Motion variables, scrub to preview, and export MP4/WebM/GIF/SVG. The handoff win: in Dev Mode → Motion a dev copies the exact timing + easing curve as CSS, JSON, or framework-ready React (motion.dev) — deleting the 'what easing did the designer mean?' guesswork. Run-it-today: switch one frame to Motion, keyframe a single button-hover or modal entrance, copy the CSS. (Open beta, all plans; Starter exports are limited; validate the exported curve against your front-end. Code Layers — code on the canvas — is separate and only rolls out in July.)

Social play (organic, free): stop guessing why a Reel popped — ask the model that has your numbers. Meta's Creator Assistant — a free conversational AI built into the Facebook creator dashboard (live since Jun 4; Meta doubled down Jun 24 with a standalone Creator Studio app built on it) — answers questions against your own account data and brainstorms ideas off live Facebook trends. The play: ask 'why did my top Reel outperform?', pull out the repeatable variable (hook/length/audio), then ask 'give me 3 angles off what's trending in my niche' and turn that into the week's posts. It beats generic best-time charts and a blank ChatGPT prompt because it sees your real performance. (Geo-gated: US/Canada/India for now; Facebook dashboard only.)

Paid play [meta-ads]: stop building one ad per format — pack ONE ad with up to 10 mixed image + video assets across aspect ratios and let Advantage+ pick the winner per placement. Meta's refreshed multimedia-ads guidance (reported Jun 24, from Meta's own help doc) is explicit: upload all your assets into a single ad and let delivery optimize — up to 10 images and videos, including multiple aspect ratios (9:16 + 1:1/4:5), in one ad. Splitting creative per placement just splinters the learning signal. The operator edge moves back to creative volume + variety: five genuinely different angles (UGC clip, demo, testimonial, text-led explainer, lifestyle) beat fifty near-duplicates. Keep Advantage+ creative on; disclose any AI-generated asset. (Per-ad cap is 10; that's distinct from total ad-set asset volume. Figure traces to Meta's doc as quoted by Social Media Today — Reported, not yet a 3-source claim.)

Also moved — the AI landscape (cross-checked; market figures directional): this is an 'AI capex is paying off' rebound week that flips the Jun 22–23 fear. Micron blew out fiscal Q3 — revenue ~$41.5B (vs ~$35.7B est) and record 84.9% gross margin, guiding Q4 to ~$50B; HBM (high-bandwidth AI memory) is sold out through 2027 — stock jumped ~+16% premarket Jun 25. OpenAI + Broadcom unveiled 'Jalapeño' (Jun 24), OpenAI's first custom inference chip (TSMC 3nm, 8 HBM stacks, deploys end-2026). Qualcomm is buying AI-compiler company Modular (~$3.9B, all-stock) to chip at Nvidia's CUDA moat. And Morgan Stanley (Jun 24) framed the selloff as a crowded dip, not broken ROI — which resolves our Jun 23 prediction ✅ (an AI-megacap/sell-side defends capex ROI within ~2 weeks). Counter-notes: Cerebras sank on a soft margin guide; Oracle shed ~21,000 roles over the year (its 10-K ties cuts to AI); and Anthropic accused Alibaba's Qwen lab of an 'illicit' model-distillation campaign (~28.8M interactions via ~25k fake accounts), sending Alibaba's HK shares ~-4.5%. For us this is a climate signal: client scrutiny is shifting from 'is AI spend justified?' to 'show me the ROI, the unit economics, and the IP provenance.'

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is stop translating between the tool that designs and the tool that ships. Figma Motion deletes the After-Effects-to-CSS hop; Creator Assistant deletes the 'pull analytics, guess the why' hop; Meta's one-ad-many-assets deletes the build-an-ad-per-placement hop. Each move collapses a handoff and hands the optimizing to the system that already has the data — then you aim the reclaimed hour at the next lever. And the market just repriced that same instinct: it has stopped paying for AI spend on faith and now wants the outcome.

sections.h: Web design — run it today: animate on the Figma canvas with Figma Motion, then copy the CSS/React easing from Dev Mode (no After Effects)

blocks.sub: Figma shipped Figma Motion at Config 2026 (Jun 24 2026): a real animation TIMELINE as a fourth canvas mode (Design / Draw / Dev / Motion) — keyframe position/scale/rotation/opacity, fade/move/scale presets, custom easing as reusable Motion variables, scrub-to-preview, export MP4/WebM/GIF/SVG — and a Dev Mode → Motion tab that copies the exact timing + easing as CSS, JSON, or framework-ready React (motion.dev)
blocks.p: What changed: at Config 2026 on Jun 24 2026 Figma shipped Figma Motion, a real animation timeline that lives on the design canvas as a fourth mode alongside Design, Draw and Dev. You keyframe position, scale, rotation and opacity independently, drag layers to retime them, and either set keyframes by hand on the playhead or let auto-keyframing record changes as you scrub. For a fast start there are fade / move / scale presets you stack to play together or sequence; custom easing curves can be saved as Motion variables so every animation that references one updates at once. You scrub the timeline to preview and can leave time-based comments on a specific moment. It exports to MP4, WebM, GIF and (animated) SVG. The handoff payoff: in Dev Mode a Motion tab surfaces every timing value, easing curve and keyframe and lets a developer copy the animation as CSS, JSON, or framework-ready React (motion.dev). Why it matters for us: micro-interaction polish — button hovers, modal entrances, page transitions — is on nearly every site we ship, and today it means rebuilding the design in After Effects/Principle/Framer and then hand-translating the result into code, with the classic 'what easing did the designer actually mean?' loss at handoff. Motion lets the designer animate where they already design, and the dev copy the exact curve from the same file — collapsing the translation step that quietly eats hours and introduces drift.
blocks.doIt: (1) Open a file you can edit, select the frame to animate, and switch it to Motion mode (next to Design/Draw/Dev) — the timeline appears alongside the canvas. (2) Fast start: apply a preset (fade, move, or scale); stack several on the timeline to play together, or drag to sequence. (3) Refine: move the playhead and keyframe position / scale / rotation / opacity independently, or rely on auto-keyframing; drag layers on the timeline to adjust timing. (4) Reuse easing: save a custom curve as a Motion variable and reference it everywhere. (5) Preview by scrubbing; leave a time-based comment to flag a moment for a teammate. (6) Hand off: open Dev Mode → Motion and copy the animation as CSS (or JSON / React), or export the clip — then validate the exported easing/keyframes against your real front-end before shipping. Card: card-webdesign-figma-motion.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (3) — Figma's own blog ('Introducing Figma Motion' + the Config 2026 recap, primary/vendor) plus two independent press confirmations (TechCrunch and The Next Web both on the Jun 24 Config 2026 animations + code-layers news). Honest scope: Motion is open beta, available on all plans, but Starter exports are limited and full design-system integration + agent-assisted motion generation favor paid seats — so treat it as a workflow you can start today, with export gating to check on free plans. The Dev Mode CSS/React copy is vendor-claimed fidelity; eyeball the exported curve before trusting it on the critical path. Distinct from Code Layers ('code on the canvas'), which is NOT a run-it-today play — it begins rolling out in July 2026 via a waitlist. Joins the design-tooling/handoff branch (related to the Figma Dev Mode MCP design↔code card and the scroll-triggered-animations CSS card — the same 'design→production motion' story). Logged a prediction (a 2nd major design tool ships an on-canvas animation timeline that exports production motion code, OR Figma Motion exits open beta to GA). Sources: Figma Blog ×2, TechCrunch, The Next Web.

sections.h: Social (organic) — run it today: ask Facebook's free Creator Assistant why your best Reel won, then have it pitch 3 angles off live trends

blocks.sub: Meta's Creator Assistant — a free conversational AI built into the Facebook creator dashboard (live since Jun 4 2026; Meta doubled down Jun 24 with a standalone Creator Studio app built on the same assistant) — answers questions against your OWN account data (why a post popped, when to post, how your audience shifted, what comments say) and brainstorms content ideas off live Facebook trends
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: Meta's Creator Assistant, a free conversational AI for organic creators built into the Facebook creator dashboard, went live on Jun 4 2026 for creators in the US, Canada and India — and on Jun 24 2026 Meta doubled down, announcing a standalone Creator Studio companion app built around the same assistant (the app itself is in limited testing / waitlist; the assistant is the part you can use now). The assistant answers questions against your own account performance — why a specific Reel outperformed, when your audience is most active, how your audience has shifted over time, what people are saying in your comments — and brainstorms fresh content angles off live Facebook trends (trending audio, cultural moments, top-performing content styles), tailored to your niche, with conversational follow-ups. Why it matters for us: most 'AI for social' advice is a blank ChatGPT prompt that knows nothing about your account, or a generic best-time-to-post chart. A first-party coach that sees your real numbers turns 'I think the hook worked' into a diagnosed, repeatable variable — and one diagnosed win, drilled into hook/length/angle variations, becomes a week of posts. It's free, organic (no ad spend), and lives next to where the content already posts.
blocks.doIt: (1) Check eligibility: you need a creator account in the US, Canada or India; open the Facebook mobile app → creator dashboard and look for the assistant (no separate download). (2) Diagnose a winner: ask 'Why did [this specific Reel] outperform the rest?' and extract the repeatable variable (hook, length, audio, topic). (3) Read your audience: ask 'How has my audience changed over time?' and 'What are people saying in my comments?' to find a new segment or sentiment to lean into. (4) Time it: ask 'When should I post?' — it answers from your own engagement patterns, not a generic chart. (5) Brainstorm off trends: ask for fresh angles tied to what's trending for your niche; capture 3. (6) Iterate: follow up ('give me 3 hook variations for that angle') to spin one insight into a week of posts; A/B the cuts and export a brand PDF in Edits (card-social-edits-creator-analytics). Card: card-social-facebook-creator-assistant.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (3) — Meta Newsroom (about.fb.com, primary, Jun 4) + TechCrunch (Jun 4 launch and Jun 24 companion-app coverage) + Social Media Today (Jun 24 Creator Studio relaunch). Honest scope: geo-gated — live only in US/Canada/India right now (more markets 'over the coming months'), so verify region before relying on it; it lives in the Facebook creator dashboard (NOT inside Instagram, despite some headlines conflating the two); the standalone Creator Studio app is waitlist-only (the assistant is the run-it-today piece, the app is not). Meta describes the access path and example questions but doesn't publish a numbered click-path, so the exact prompts above are assembled from Meta's described capabilities — adapt to what your dashboard surfaces. Advances the Instagram/creator-tooling thread (the editor/dashboard becomes the analytics-and-ideas desk); related: card-social-edits-creator-analytics, card-social-instagram-your-algorithm, card-social-repurpose-claude. Logged a prediction (Creator Assistant expands beyond US/CA/India OR the standalone Creator Studio app exits waitlist by Q3-end). Sources: Meta Newsroom, TechCrunch ×2, Social Media Today.

sections.h: Paid — run it today: pack ONE Meta ad with up to 10 mixed image + video assets across aspect ratios, and let Advantage+ pick the winner per placement

blocks.sub: Meta's refreshed multimedia-ads guidance (reported Jun 24 2026, from Meta's own 'multi-media ads' help doc): stop splitting creative into a separate ad per placement — upload up to 10 images and videos, including multiple aspect ratios (9:16 + 1:1/4:5), into a single ad and let Advantage+ delivery optimize which variation each user sees
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: Meta published refreshed best-practice guidance for its multimedia (multi-media) ads, written up Jun 24 2026 and traced to Meta's own help doc. The core instruction: upload all your assets into a single ad and let Meta handle delivery automatically — specifically up to 10 images and videos per ad, and you can include assets in different aspect ratios (e.g. 1:1 and 9:16) in that same ad. Meta's stated logic: 'the more creative options you provide, the more opportunities the delivery system has to optimize your delivery and performance,' picking the best-fitting variation per placement and user. Why it matters for us: operators still routinely build a separate ad per aspect ratio/placement, which splinters the conversion signal across many ad objects and fragments delivery. Consolidating diverse assets into one ad concentrates that signal and hands Meta's Andromeda-era delivery a richer combination space to optimize against — and it shifts the lever back to the thing you actually control: creative volume and variety, not audience micro-targeting. This is a creative-architecture move (how you pack assets inside the ad), distinct from the Advantage+ campaign-consolidation play (how you structure the campaign).
blocks.doIt: (1) In Ads Manager, create/open an ad and choose the Advantage+ / multimedia creative setup. (2) At the ad (creative) level, upload up to 10 assets into the one ad — deliberately mix at least one video with several images. (3) Include multiple aspect ratios in that single ad: 9:16 (vertical — most inventory) plus 1:1 or 4:5 for feed. (4) Make the assets conceptually distinct, not minor variants — e.g. a UGC clip, a product demo, a testimonial, a text-led explainer, a lifestyle shot (~5 genuinely different angles beats 50 near-duplicates). (5) Keep Advantage+ creative delivery ON so Meta auto-selects per placement/user; design within placement safe zones so key visuals survive cropping. (6) If any asset is AI-generated, set Meta's AI-content disclosure before launch; refresh assets every 2–4 weeks, watching CPM/reach as the fatigue signal. Card: card-paid-meta-multimedia-creative.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Reported (2) — Meta's own 'multi-media ads' help doc (primary; the page loads and matches, though its body text wasn't machine-extractable) + Social Media Today (Jun 24, which quotes the doc for the '10 assets / mixed aspect ratios' figure); the broader 'diverse creative volume, let delivery optimize' strategy is independently echoed by agency sources (AdMove), pushing the general play toward Confirmed. Honest scope: the hard figure is up to 10 assets PER AD — don't conflate it with the larger asset-volume targets agencies recommend at the ad-set level (those are recommendations, not a Meta limit); this is creative best-practice guidance, not a dated product launch; and AI-content disclosure has been required since Mar 2026. Advances the platform-AI-defaults thread (related: card-paid-meta-advantage-plus, card-paid-ai-ad-creative, card-graphic-firefly-brand-batch-creatives). Logged a prediction (Meta raises the per-ad asset cap above 10 OR makes multimedia/multi-aspect-ratio the default creative setup). Sources: Meta for Business Help, Social Media Today, AdMove. Scan-line (not a card): Google began rolling out the June 2026 Spam Update globally Jun 24 (organic SEO — watch Search Console for ranking shifts), and is testing US-only auto-applied 'Strongest match' labels on Search ads (no advertiser controls yet — a watch-line, not runnable).

sections.h: News — the AI landscape (cross-checked; all market figures directional, premarket/intraday vs close vary by source)

blocks.sub: 'AI capex is paying off' rebound flips the Jun 22–23 fear: Micron blew out fiscal Q3 (rev ~$41.5B vs ~$35.7B est, record 84.9% gross margin, Q4 guide ~$50B; HBM sold out through 2027) and jumped ~+16% premarket Jun 25 — and Morgan Stanley reframed the selloff as a 'crowded dip, not broken ROI,' RESOLVING our Jun 23 prediction ✅
blocks.p: What moved: this is decisively an 'AI-capex-is-paying-off' rebound week, flipping the Jun 22–23 global tech rout. Micron reported fiscal Q3 2026 revenue of ~$41.5B (vs ~$35.7B expected, ~16% beat), adjusted EPS ~$25.1 (vs ~$20.5), a record 84.9% gross margin, and guided Q4 to ~$50B revenue / ~86% margin — with data-center revenue ~$25B in the quarter, HBM4 shipments already >$1B, and HBM (high-bandwidth memory, the chips that feed AI accelerators) sold out through 2027 into 2028; the stock jumped ~+16% premarket Jun 25 (directional; after-hours prints ranged +13–15%). On the chip side, OpenAI + Broadcom unveiled 'Jalapeño' (Jun 24), OpenAI's first custom AI accelerator — a reticle-sized ASIC purpose-built for LLM inference (not training), TSMC 3nm, systolic-array design with eight HBM stacks, engineering samples already running workloads, initial deployment targeted for end-2026. And Qualcomm announced an all-stock acquisition of Modular (~$3.9B; the Mojo/MAX AI-compiler company founded by LLVM/Swift creator Chris Lattner — an explicit play at Nvidia's CUDA software moat, NOT a chip startup as some feeds framed it). The repricing question itself got answered: Morgan Stanley (Andrew Slimmon, Jun 24) framed the Jun 22–23 drop as AI beneficiaries being 'not expensive, but crowded' with the 'earnings-revision story validated' — a major sell-side note defending the AI thesis as a crowded dip, not broken ROI. Why it matters for us: this is a climate signal, not a trade. Two weeks ago the market punished AI on 'is the spend justified?'; this week the supply chain is visibly monetizing it. Client scrutiny shifts accordingly — from 'why are we spending on AI?' to 'show me the ROI, the unit economics (cost per outcome / perf-per-watt), and the IP provenance.' Prediction resolved ✅ [news, made 2026-06-23, due ~Jul 7]: 'within ~2 weeks, ≥1 US AI-megacap team or major sell-side note explicitly defends AI-capex ROI in response to the Jun 22–23 selloff' — the Morgan Stanley note (with Micron's call as supporting evidence) clears it. Corroboration: Micron beat + guide Confirmed (≥3 — Investing.com earnings-call transcripts, Futurum, CNBC); Jalapeño Confirmed (≥5 — OpenAI, Broadcom investor release, TechCrunch, CNBC, SiliconANGLE); Qualcomm–Modular Confirmed (≥3 — Yahoo Finance, Network World, Wired); Morgan Stanley note (Yahoo Finance). All percentages directional. Sources: Micron transcript (Investing.com), Broadcom/OpenAI, CNBC, Yahoo Finance.

blocks.sub: Counter-notes keep it honest: Cerebras sank on a soft margin guide (CEO says investors 'misunderstood' it), Oracle shed ~21,000 roles over the year (its 10-K ties cuts to AI), and Anthropic accused Alibaba's Qwen lab of an 'illicit' model-distillation campaign — sending Alibaba's HK shares ~-4.5% and surfacing AI-IP provenance as a live risk
blocks.p: The rebound is selective, not blanket. Cerebras (Nasdaq: CBRS, IPO'd May 2026) posted first-as-public earnings — revenue $193M (+94% YoY), loss narrowed — but the stock fell ~17–20% (directional) because FY gross-margin guidance of 38–41% undershot its ~47% Q1 core margin; CEO Andrew Feldman told CNBC investors 'misunderstood' the guide, attributing it to temporarily renting back its own systems from a big customer while it builds capacity, not pricing pressure. Oracle's annual 10-K shows headcount down to 141,000 from ~162,000 — roughly 21,000 roles (~13%) cut over fiscal 2026 — with the filing explicitly tying reductions to 'adoption and deployment of AI technologies,' even as capex surged ~162% to $55.7B for AI data-center buildout (the 'AI pays off for owners, not always for workers' edge of the same story). And on AI-IP: Anthropic accused operators linked to Alibaba's Qwen lab of the largest known 'distillation' campaign against it — ~28.8M interactions via ~25,000 fraudulent accounts (Apr 22–Jun 5) to 'illicitly' extract Claude's coding/agentic-reasoning capability — in a letter to US senators and the White House; Alibaba's HK shares (9988.HK) fell ~4.5% (directional) to a ~16-month low (YTD ~-33%), and Alibaba had not commented as of Jun 25 (these are Anthropic's allegations, untested). Why it matters for us: 'distillation' (training a cheaper model on a frontier model's outputs) becoming a public, geopolitical fight is the IP-provenance lesson going mainstream — expect clients (and procurement) to start asking where a model's capability came from and whether your AI deliverables are built on a defensible base. Thread note: the V-shape 'dip not regime change' call [news, due ~Jul 20] is now leaning held — the Asian AI basket sits well above its Jun 8 reference (Samsung ~+21%, SK Hynix ~+53% to a record; SoftBank the lone laggard ~-7%); still open. Apple [due ~Jul 21] is still below its Jun 8 pre-WWDC level (~-3%) — open, currently trailing. Corroboration: Cerebras Confirmed (≥3 — TechCrunch, CNBC, Investing.com); Oracle Confirmed (≥3 — Yahoo Finance, CNBC, 10-K); Anthropic–Alibaba Confirmed (≥3 — Reuters, Investing.com, Bloomberg/CNBC), allegations untested. Sources: TechCrunch, Yahoo Finance, Reuters.
